coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a novel nanomaterial, offer new avenues for structural health monitoring owing to the remarkable fluorescence response. In this study, the GQDs-epoxy resin composites were fabricated and applied to structural surface. Tests including tension, compression, bending, and fatigue were conducted to explore the fluorescence response of composites to structural strain and cracks. Results show that composites exhibited a heightened fluorescence sensitivity to structural strain. During the tensile tests of coated steel specimens, the fluorescence intensity of composites exhibited positive and negative correlation responses with increasing strain. Whereas in the compression test of coated concrete specimens, solely increased fluorescence intensity with rising strain was observed. Composites demonstrated excellent crack characterization on concrete and steel structures, whether penetrating or developing cracks. The fluorescence response of composites to cracks was based on the fluorescence enhancement at the crack site, and fluorescence intensity was approximately inversely proportional to crack width.

head.ce:title: Structural strain and crack monitoring based on the fluorescence response of graphene quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a novel nanomaterial, offer new avenues for structural health monitoring owing to the remarkable fluorescence response. In this study, the GQDs-epoxy resin composites were fabricated and applied to structural surface. Tests including tension, compression, bending, and fatigue were conducted to explore the fluorescence response of composites to structural strain and cracks. Results show that composites exhibited a heightened fluorescence sensitivity to structural strain. During the tensile tests of coated steel specimens, the fluorescence intensity of composites exhibited positive and negative correlation responses with increasing strain. Whereas in the compression test of coated concrete specimens, solely increased fluorescence intensity with rising strain was observed. Composites demonstrated excellent crack characterization on concrete and steel structures, whether penetrating or developing cracks. The fluorescence response of composites to cracks was based on the fluorescence enhancement at the crack site, and fluorescence intensity was approximately inversely proportional to crack width.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: As society advances, structural systems are evolving towards larger scale, greater height, and increased refinement. Alongside this progress, safety concerns are becoming more prominent, with structural cracks emerging as a focal point of scholarly attention. The presence of structural cracks can significantly impact the safety, durability, and aesthetic appeal of the structure, and in severe cases, may lead to its overall failure [1–3].

For concrete structures, prolonged exposure to harsh environmental conditions such as corrosion, frost, and chloride ion infiltration can result in surface cracks and other forms of damage. Continued operation of concrete structures with cracks not only affects their load-bearing capacity but also accelerates the corrosion of harmful ions to internal reinforcements, posing significant safety risks [4,5]. Similarly, steel structures are susceptible to fatigue cracking due to the combined effects of long-term cyclic loading and manufacturing imperfections. Once fatigue cracks propagate from microcracks to longer ones, they can drastically shorten the structural service life and even lead to catastrophic collapse [6,7]. Early detection and remediation of structural cracks are essential prerequisites for effectively preventing serious structural damage.

At present, within the realm of civil engineering, a variety of structural health monitoring sensors are available, with a relatively mature research framework. Common non-destructive detection and monitoring methods for structures encompass ultrasonic imaging [8], X-ray scanning [9], acoustic emission testing [10], piezoelectric-based methods [11,12], resistance-based methods [13–15], digital image correlation (DIC) [16–20], and so on. Among these, the method centered on resistance change holds particular appeal for scholars due to its inherent real-time capabilities and cost-effectiveness compared to alternative methods. Hallaji et al. [21,22] utilized electrical resistance tomography to monitor localized alterations in the resistivity of the sensing skin, enabling the detection and localization of concrete cracks and damage. Zhang et al. [23] investigated the correlation between crack width and the ultimate strain of film break wires, employing detection of copper wire breakage to quantify the width of structural cracks. However, these sensors rely on the response of resistance changes to monitor structural integrity. The processing and transformation of collected resistance data often necessitate complex procedures, and the characterization effect also needs to be improved.

Tochaei et al. [24] established a monitoring system for the health of steel bridges utilizing fiber grating sensors to assess the fatigue performance of welded joints. Wang et al. [25] discovered that the coupling capacitance increased with the increase of dielectric constant of enameled wire sensors, resulting in elevated coupling voltage and impacting crack response assessment. Dey et al. [26,27] introduced a wireless, cost-effective, and wide-coverage sensing system employing time domain reflectometry technology to detect structural cracks. Meng et al. [28] fabricated a sensor by blending graphene nanoplatelets with epoxy resin via ultrasonic treatment, possessing self-inductive properties capable of detecting strain changes and monitoring dynamic motion with heightened sensitivity to bending and twisting. Furthermore, Alam et al. [29] utilized acoustic emission and DIC methodologies to discern crack openings and fractures in concrete beams, effectively identifying fracture process zones and cracking mechanisms. Al-Salih et al. [17] successfully characterized the propagation of cracks in steel beam under fatigue load by DIC technology.

Despite the maturity of these techniques, they still face challenges such as response time, potential sensors damage, and cost constraints. Some wired crack sensors for structural monitoring often rely on cables for signal transmission, while wireless alternatives typically necessitate batteries for power [30]. Based on the optical response properties of fluorescent quantum dots, quantum dot fluorescence sensors offer a promising avenue for mitigating these limitations to some degree.

Quantum dots are fluorescent semiconductor nanoparticles crafted from heavy metals or inorganic substances, whose recognition and sensing of fluorescence signals emitted have emerged as pivotal research avenues, garnering significant attention across diverse fields such as biology and aerospace [31,32]. Through investigations in various quantum dot properties, scholars found that exposing quantum dots to different pressures induced shifts in photoluminescence color, resulting in either blue or red shifts in fluorescence peaks [33], alongside corresponding fluctuations in fluorescence intensity [34]. Additionally, the photoelectric characteristics of quantum dots exhibited distinct responses to mechanical strain [35,36]. Currently, some scholars have carried out research on quantum dot-coated sensors. Ford et al. [37] presented a CdSe/ZnS core/shell quantum dots-epoxy nanocomposite, and its spectral intensity response to pressure can be used to detect pressure changes. Yin et al. [38] developed a quantum dot-based composite, and its photoluminescence intensity would change significantly to large engineering strain. Moreover, scholars also studied the coating of quantum dots-epoxy resin film on metal materials to monitor the crack propagation of materials in real time [39,40].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are relatively excellent in quantum dots. As a zero-dimensional structure of graphene, GQDs boast adjustable photoluminescence, excellent water solubility, minimal biological toxicity, stable and bright fluorescence, facile surface modification, and chemical stability [41,42]. He et al. [43] synthesized a range of nano-quantum dot graphene/titanium oxide materials via the sol–gel method, utilizing them for modified asphalt and investigating the resistance to the ultraviolet (UV) aging of modified asphalt. Xie et al. [44] incorporated tungsten oxide and tungsten sulfide quantum dots into graphene oxide/titanium dioxide composites, observing enhanced light absorption, conductivity, and specific surface area in the quantum dot-modified composites. Win et al. [45] demonstrated that cement composites containing GQDs exhibited superior preservation, mechanical, and sulfate attack resistance properties. However, the development and research of the GQDs fluorescent-coated sensors in structural engineering remain unexplored at present. Hence, developing a GQDs composite film-coated sensor to monitor structural strain and cracks based on the excellent fluorescence response of GQDs holds significant value for the health monitoring of civil engineering structures.

In this study, the GQDs-epoxy resin composites were formulated by integrating GQDs with epoxy resin. The correlation between structural strain and fluorescence response was established through a series of tests including tensile, compression, bending, and fatigue, elucidating the structural strain-fluorescence response mechanism across micro and macroscopic scales. Subsequently, the characteristics and fluorescence response of the composite materials to the cracks were investigated, elucidating the fluorescence response mechanism of the GQDs-epoxy composite materials to crack formation and propagation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites
Introduction of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs epoxy resin composite materials are advanced composite materials with multiple functions formed by embedding GQDs into epoxy resin. GQDs have quantum confinement effect and excellent photoelectric properties. Their unique electronic structure allows them to absorb and re-emit photons, with fluorescence intensity and wavelength adapting to environmental changes. These properties of GQDs endow the GQDs-epoxy resin composites with superior photoluminescence and fluorescence characteristics, making them highly suitable for sensitive optoelectronic applications such as advanced light-emitting devices and sensors. The epoxy resin serves as an ideal carrier matrix, ensuring an even distribution of GQDs throughout the composites. It also provides robust structural support and adhesion, allowing the composites to be uniformly coated on the structural surfaces. Moreover, epoxy resin enhances the durability and environmental stability of the composites, maintaining their mechanical strength and chemical resistance.

The even dispersion of GQDs within the epoxy resin results in outstanding strain-sensing capabilities for the composites. When the composites are subjected to external load, changes in the fluorescence response of GQDs can be detected, enabling real-time strain monitoring. Additionally, the composites can effectively track structural cracks, as crack formation alters the concentration of GQDs in the vicinity, leading to noticeable changes in fluorescence, which allows the composites for precise crack detection and severity assessment. Overall, the GQDs-epoxy resin composites hold significant promise for structural health monitoring by merging the photoluminescent and fluorescent properties of GQDs with the structural integrity provided by epoxy resin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since the different preparation processes will lead to different fluorescence intensity, color and spectrum of GQDs, it is vital to select the appropriate preparation process to prepare GQDs with excellent performance for use as the fluorescence sensor of the composite material. Three types of GQDs were prepared in the laboratory according to the procedure reported by Ghaffarkhah et al. [46]. As depicted in Fig. 1 , the prepared GQDs had uniform particle size and were easy to disperse under electron microscope.

The fluorescence characteristics of three types of GQDs were measured. The GQDs were mixed into anhydrous ethanol at a consistent concentration (30 mg/ml), fully stirred and ultrasonically dispersed for 5 min to prepare the GQDs solution. After 365 nm UV irradiation, the fluorescence spectra of the GQDs solution were measured by fluorescence fiber spectrometer. As depicted in Fig. 2 , three GQDs showed blue, green and pale blue fluorescence, respectively. Specifically, GQDs1 emitted fluorescence at a wavelength of 501 nm with an intensity of 51,713 Counts, displaying a smooth spectrum with a single fluorescence peak. GQDs2 emitted fluorescence at 483 nm with an intensity of 51,303 Counts, characterized by a spectrum with two peaks, where the second peak was higher than the first. GQDs3 exhibited a lower fluorescence intensity of 38,075 Counts, indicating poor fluorescence performance unsuitable for the fluorescence response monitoring. So GQDs1 and GQDs2 were selected for the preparation and subsequent testing of GQDs-epoxy resin composites.

Given the difficulty in employing GQDs directly for practical monitoring purposes, high transparent epoxy resin was chosen as the carrier matrix of GQDs, which served the dual purpose of shielding GQDs from oxidation while maintaining their fluorescence characteristics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solution mixing approach
ce:section.ce:para: The solution mixing approach is widely employed for preparing composite materials from nano-materials and organic compounds. Typically, this approach involves two main steps. Initially, GQDs are dissolved in a solvent and then uniformly dispersed through mechanical stirring and ultrasonic dispersion. Subsequently, the uniformly dispersed GQDs solution is introduced into epoxy resin, where further stirring and ultrasonic treatment ensure thorough dispersion.

Epoxy resin, due to its high viscosity and low internal shear stress, presents challenges for achieving uniform dispersion of GQDs using conventional dispersion techniques such as mechanical or electromagnetic stirring. However, adjusting the dispersion temperature and incorporating solvents with high solubility can decrease epoxy resin viscosity and internal shear stress. From prior experiments, anhydrous ethanol was identified as the optimal solvent for GQDs. It effectively dissolves GQDs, exhibits compatibility with epoxy resin, and minimally impacts the strength post-volatilization of cured resin. To achieve optimal fluorescence performance, the optimal concentration of GQDs solution was determined to be 40 mg/ml. When the concentration exceeds 40 mg/ml, the fluorescence intensity decreases, i.e., the fluorescence quenching of quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation process of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 depicts the detailed preparation process of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites. Initially, the requisite amount of GQDs for the experiment was dissolved in anhydrous ethanol at a concentration of 40 mg/ml. Subsequently, the solution underwent mechanical stirring for 3 min. Following this, the GQDs solution was transferred to ultrasonic disperser for 5 min of dispersion to ensure effective dispersion, thereby facilitating the complete dissolution of the agglomerated GQDs in the solution. Next, the prepared GQDs solution, highly transparent epoxy resin and curing agent were mixed in a volume ratio of 0.9: 2.5: 1, and the mixture was stirred with a glass rod for 5 min to achieve thorough integration of the three components. Then the stirred solution underwent an additional 5 min of dispersion in ultrasonic disperser to ensure uniform dispersion of GQDs within the epoxy resin. This step was crucial for maintaining consistent fluorescence intensity throughout the GQDs-epoxy resin composite. At last, the dispersed solution was placed in a drying box with a temperature of 85 °C for curing. After curing for 2 h and cooling to room temperature, the cured GQDS-epoxy resin composites can be obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tensile strain monitoring by the GQDs-epoxy resin composites
ce:section.ce:para: Major infrastructure like bridges, tunnels, etc., are basically endure multiple loading and unloading throughout their life time service [6,7]. Consequently, it is crucial to study the fluorescence response of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites to multiple tensile loading and unloading, and ascertain the capability to maintain consistent fluorescence synchronization under such conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of tensile coated steel specimens
ce:section.ce:para: The dimensions of tensile steel specimens adhere to the guidelines outlined in the Chinese standard GB/T 228.1–2010 [47], the specimen size is shown in Fig. 4 (a). To ensure the representativeness of test, Q235 steel, widely utilized in building structures, was chosen as the material for specimens. Initially, the surface of the steel specimen was scrubbed with alcohol to eliminate the surface stains, ensuring a clean and smooth surface. Subsequently, the composite materials mixed solution, prepared following the procedures outlined in Section 2.4, was applied to the gauge section (60 mm) of the steel specimen, as shown the bule area in Fig. 4(a). Utilizing the gravity of liquid, a uniform and smooth film with a thickness of 0.3 mm was formed. After being placed in a drying box for 2 h, the film can be cured on the tensile specimen, as depicted in Fig. 4(b). Upon irradiation with 365 nm UV light, the coating film on the steel specimen exhibited a vibrant blue fluorescence, as depicted in Fig. 4(c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tensile test device and loading scheme
ce:section.ce:para: To collect the relationship between tensile strain and the fluorescence response of GQDs-epoxy resin composites in real time during the test process, a fluorescence response acquisition system was built as depicted in Fig. 5 (b). The fluorescence fiber optic spectrometer (FLAME-S-XR1 produced by Ocean Optics was used, with wavelength from 200 nm to 1.025 μm) can accurately measure the fluorescence spectrum of the specimen and collect the rapidly changing fluorescence intensity in real time. As the excitation light source, the LED 365 nm UV visible light can well excite the fluorescence of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites and facilitate the collection of fluorescence data. The Y-type fiber was chosen for the connection, featuring an outer layer with six illumination fibers and a middle layer with one readout fiber. The readout fiber had diameters of 200 μm and 500 μm. One end can be connected to the fluorescence spectrometer and the excitation light source at the same time, and the other end can directly collect the fluorescence spectrum of the specimen, as depicted in Fig. 5(a). The fiber spectrometer was connected to computer, and the fluorescence spectrum collected by the spectrometer can be observed in real time through the operation software, then the corresponding parameters were set to measure and save the fluorescence intensity and fluorescence spectrum. The tripod was employed to fix the acquisition end of the Y-type optical fiber, which can adjust the displacement of the X, Y and Z directions, so as to maintain good mobility when collecting the fluorescence signal.

The multiple tensile loading and unloading test of the steel specimen coated with the composite film were conducted employing the universal material testing machine (UMTM) (model UTM5305SLXY, measuring range 300kN) depicted in Fig. 5(c). The steel specimen was installed in the fixture on both sides, and the two ends of the specimen were clamped. Loading and unloading procedures can be realized by adjusting the fixture positioned above. Throughout the test process, the steel specimen remained within its elastic limit. Force-controlled loading was employed, with a loading rate set at 50 N/s, and the fluorescence intensity was captured once every 400 N. Until reaching a load of 6 kN, the loading ceased and was maintained for 10 s to ensure load stability, facilitating fluorescence intensity data collection. Upon unloading to 100 N, the unloading process paused for 10 s.

It is important to highlight that during each measurement of the specimen, the optical fiber probe must maintain a consistent distance from the specimen being measured to ensure the accuracy and representativeness of the collected fluorescence spectrum data. After numerous trials, it was determined that maintaining a distance between optical fiber probe and specimen within the range of 0.5 to 1 cm guaranteed the precision of the measured fluorescence spectrum and enabled the effective capture of real-time changes in the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tensile test results and discussion
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites in the single loading and unloading test of steel specimen coated with composites was explored as depicted in Fig. 6 . The fluorescence intensity of the composites changed in two scenarios with the increase of the steel specimen strain during the test. One scenario was that fluorescence intensity exhibited a positive correlation with the steel specimen strain (Fig. 6(a)), indicating that fluorescence intensity increased with the increasing strain and decreased with the decreasing strain. When the strain reached its maximum value, the fluorescence intensity of the composites also peaked. The other scenario was that fluorescence intensity displayed a negative correlation with the steel specimen strain (Fig. 6(b)), wherein fluorescence intensity decreased with the increasing strain while increased with the decreasing strain. The analysis of the tensile fluorescence response mechanism in Section 3.4 can elucidate these two distinct scenarios.

During multiple cyclic loading, the fluorescence response of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites mirrored that observed during single loading and unloading, and there are also two fluorescence response scenarios of positive and negative correlation, as depicted in Fig. 7 . The Q235 steel specimen remained within the elastic range throughout multiple cyclic loading. The fluorescence response curves for both positive and negative correlation were basically linear, indicating high sensitivity to the variations of steel specimen strain. Moreover, the fluorescence response curves depicting positive and negative correlation demonstrated robust synchronization and stability within the initial three cycles. However, the subsequent fluorescence response became less stable, accompanied by a gradual attenuation in fluorescence intensity. Whereas, the characteristic trends corresponding to the increase and decrease of strain still persisted. This instability can be attributed to several factors. Firstly, epoxy resin, as a viscoelastic material, accumulated residual strain over multiple loading and unloading cycles. Secondly, the bonding between the GQDs-epoxy resin composites and steel specimen weakened under repeated cyclic loads, disrupting their synchronization and contributing to the instability of fluorescence response. Thirdly, the occurrence of fluorescence drift phenomena exacerbated instability in subsequent cycles, resulting in a decline in overall fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tensile fluorescence response mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 depicts the two fluorescence intensity response scenarios observed in the GQDS-epoxy resin composites subjected to tensile strain. In Scenario 1, as the structural tensile strain increases, the strain on the composite film surface increases correspondingly. This leads to a reduction in the number of GQDs per unit area, resulting in a decrease in concentration and subsequently, a decline in fluorescence intensity under the UV light irradiation. This scenario represents the predominant phenomenon observed in the experiment. Conversely, in Scenario 2, escalating tensile strain triggers the interaction between the epoxy resin and internal GQDs, causing the agglomerated GQDs to separate. Consequently, the number of GQDs exposed to UV light per unit area increases, leading to a higher concentration and an elevation in fluorescence intensity. This scenario is commonly observed under lower tensile strain conditions. These two scenarios elucidate the positive and negative correlation between fluorescence intensity and structural tensile strain as depicted in Figs. 6–7.

It is worth noting that the occurrence of the second scenario primarily stems from the formation of agglomerated GQDs caused by the randomness of dispersion. In practical engineering applications, it is necessary to further enhance the dispersion techniques for GQDs to minimize agglomeration and ensure their uniform distribution within the epoxy resin. By achieving this, the fluorescence intensity response of the GQDS-epoxy resin composites will only exhibit a positive correlation with structural tensile strain, thereby securing the stability of the fluorescence response for practical implementations.

To better visualize the GQDs distribution within epoxy resin under tensile stress, Yin et al. [38] conducted a detailed micro-level investigation. They integrated CdSe@ZnS core–shell quantum dots into epoxy resin to create a composite material, subsequently made into a tensile specimen. The distribution change of quantum dots caused by tensile strain in internal core–shell quantum dots was investigated through tensile test. Fig. 9 presents the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images captured from a 280 × 425 nm2 area of the tensile specimen under 0 N and 900 N of tensile force. Fig. 9(a)–(b) correspond to the Scenario 1 in Fig. 8, where increasing tensile strain led to a reduction in the number of quantum dots per unit area, resulting in diminished fluorescence intensity. Fig. 9(c)–(d) aligned with the Scenario 2 in Fig. 8, illustrating the formation of aggregates within the prepared tensile specimens containing quantum dots. These aggregates underwent tensile deformation, causing a slight dispersion of quantum dots from the center, exposing more quantum dots to UV light and consequently increasing fluorescence intensity. From Figs. 8–9, it became apparent that the fluctuations in the number of quantum dots per unit area could be a primary factor influencing changes in the fluorescence intensity of the composite material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Compressive strain monitoring of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites
ce:section.ce:para: In practical engineering applications, experiencing solely tensile strain is impractical, and the occurrence of compression-induced strain is unavoidable [48]. Consequently, there is a need for additional exploration into the fluorescence behavior of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites under compression-induced strain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of compression coated concrete specimen
ce:section.ce:para: Four sets of compression tests were devised for standard concrete cubes. Each set corresponded to a specific concrete strength grade: C20, C30, C40, and C50. For each grade, three cubes measuring 100 mm × 100 mm × 100 mm were fabricated. The prepared test blocks were placed in a curing room and subjected to standard curing conditions for a duration of 28 days.

As depicted in Fig. 10 (a), the GQDs-epoxy resin composites were applied at the center of the concrete cube surface, forming a coating with approximately 0.5 mm thickness covering an area of 60 mm × 60 mm. The distance between each side of the coating and the concrete cube edge was 20 mm. Prior to coating, foam glue was applied to the concrete cube surface, leaving the central 60 mm × 60 mm area exposed. The GQDs-epoxy resin composite solution, prepared according to Section 2.4, was preheated at 60 °C for 5 min to enhance fluidity. The preheated composite solution was absorbed by a disposable dropper with a volume of 2 ml, and slowly dispensed onto the coating area, ensuring complete coverage facilitated by the self-leveling properties of the solution. After curing, the surrounding foam glue was removed and smooth-surfaced coated concrete cubes can be obtained, depicted in Fig. 10(b)–(c). The coating positions of the composite materials on all concrete cubes were uniform, and the surface was smooth.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Compression test device and loading scheme
ce:section.ce:para: For precise structural compressive strain measurement, strain gauges of model 120-50AA were affixed onto the concrete cubes post-coating, as depicted in Fig. 11 (a). The strain gauge had a grid size of 50 mm × 3 mm and a base size of 57 mm × 5 mm. The strain acquisition box was connected to the strain gauge of the compression specimen, enabling real-time strain acquisition. The strain data collected by the strain acquisition box at a frequency of once per second. Placed the specimens in the UMTM depicted in Fig. 11(b), the side of concrete cubes coated with the GQDs-epoxy resin composites was facing outward. Concurrently, a fluorescence response acquisition system was established. The optical fiber probe was aligned to the center of the composite material, facilitating real-time collection of the fluorescence intensity variation of the composites. Given the minimal compressive strain of concrete, fluorescence intensity was gathered every 5 s to discern variations clearly. The UMTM was adopted to vertically load the specimen at a rate of 0.5 MPa/s. and testing ceased upon specimen fracture or a significant abrupt change in fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Compression test results and discussion
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the strain variations observed across four groups of concrete with varying strength grades, due to the good synchronization between concrete cube and the composite film applied to its surface, the composites generated corresponding fluorescence intensity response. Through the fluctuations in fluorescence intensity of the composites in response to the compressive strain of concrete cubes, the relationship between them was elucidated.

Fig. 12 shows the compressive strain time history curves of concrete specimens featuring varying strength grades. To ensure the representativeness of results, groups exhibiting stable early-stage growth in strain curves within each concrete strength grade were selected for discussion. Linear regression analysis was conducted on the compressive strain time history curve of each group, fitting them with elastic phase-appropriate formulas to establish a more robust relationship with the fluorescence intensity response of composites. The curves after linear regression fitting, fitting formulas, 95 % regression confidence band and 95 % prediction band are displayed in Fig. 12. Fig. 12(e) compares the strain time history curves of C20-C50 concrete post-linear regression. As the concrete strength grade increased, the slope of four lines gradually diminished, indicating a slowdown in compressive strain growth during the elastic stage and a decrease in maximum compressive strain.

To clearly compare the fluorescence intensity of the composites under different concrete strength grades, the relative fluorescence intensity was adopted for characterization, as shown in Eq. (1).
(1)
C =
C 1
C 0
where C is the relative fluorescence intensity of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites; C 0 is the initial fluorescence intensity (t = 0); C 1 is the fluorescence intensity after t = 0. Fig. 13 depicts the relative fluorescence intensity time history curves for composite materials across various concrete strength grades. The scatter plots demonstrated a predominantly linear growth in fluorescence intensity. To establish a correlation with concrete compressive strain, linear regression was applied to the scatter plots of relative fluorescence intensity for composites. Fig. 13(e) contrasts the linear regression curves for the relative fluorescence intensity of each composite material. As the concrete strength grade increased, the slope of the four lines decreased, indicating a slower increase in fluorescence intensity and a decrease in maximum fluorescence intensity, consistent with the trend observed in Fig. 12(e).

The response relationship between the compressive strain and the corresponding fluorescence intensity of composites was further investigated. As depicted in Fig. 14 , the fitted compressive strain curves closely aligned with the fitted relative fluorescence intensity curves. Over time, both the structural compressive strain and the relative fluorescence intensity of composites demonstrated an increase. During the elastic stage, the relationship between the two was predominantly linear, which illustrated that the fluctuations in fluorescence intensity induced by the composites under compressive loading were highly correlated with the changes in structural compressive strain, i.e., the GQDs-epoxy resin composites had a remarkably sensitive fluorescence response to compressive strain. Notably, in the compression test, solely the trend of increased fluorescence intensity with rising compressive strain was observed. This trend can be attributed to the escalating concentration of GQDs per unit area during compression, leading to a greater number of GQDs within the range of optical fiber probe and consequently amplifying the fluorescence intensity. Further exploration of the fluorescence response mechanism under compression will be undertaken in Section 4.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Compressed fluorescence response mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: From the experimental results detailed in Sections 4.3, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity of the composites increased alongside the compressive strain during the compression process. This phenomenon arises because the gaps between GQDs become narrower when the composites are subjected to compressive loading. Consequently, under strain induction, the number of GQDs per unit area rises, augmenting the concentration within the collection range of the fiber probe and resulting in heightened fluorescence intensity. Fig. 15 visually illustrates the macroscopic fluorescence response mechanism of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites under compressive strain.

This mechanism aligns with the findings by Phirouznia et al. [49], who noted that uniaxial stress induced caused atomic displacement when examining the linear optical properties of GQDs, which can diminish or eliminate the band gap of quantum dots due to geometric isolation. Unlike the gapless monolayer graphene, energy dispersion relation of graphene-based nanostructures like GQDs takes a different form by introducing a gap. As a result, uniaxial stress reduces or eliminates the band gap, leading to fluorescence intensity variation corresponding to changes in the quantum dot gap.

Under compressive strain, the GQDs inside composites underwent interactions with epoxy resin, which may lead to structural distortions [50] or induce a size effect, causing alterations in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs. Existing research also illustrated this point. Kim et al. [51] investigated the size dependences of the GQDs photoluminescence by changing the size and edge state of GQDs. They observed that alterations in the shape and size of GQDs led to corresponding changes in the photoluminescence (fluorescence) intensity. To a certain extent, this observation suggested that the interaction between GQDs and epoxy resin in composites, induced by stress and strain, can result in modifications to the size and morphology of composites. When irradiated by UV light, the fluorescence intensity will change.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of structural cracks by the GQDs-epoxy resin composites
ce:section.ce:para: The occurrence of cracks during the long-term service life of a structure is deemed inevitable. When these minute cracks propagate and evolve into larger cracks, the structural integrity may be severely compromised. The efficacy of current monitoring techniques in characterizing structural cracks necessitates enhancement, especially concerning the detection of minute cracks that are challenging to identify during the early stage of fatigue cracking. Therefore, a thorough investigation into the fluorescence response and characterization of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites concerning structural cracks is essential for advancing monitoring practices in structural maintenance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of concrete cracks
Design of concrete specimen
ce:section.ce:para: To induce concrete cracking, four-point bending tests were carried out on concrete prisms. Cracks could be initiated in the concrete under a minor load. In accordance with the Chinese standard GB/T 50081-2019 [52], the concrete prisms measured 100 mm × 100 mm × 400 mm, with a strength grade of C30. Following preparation, the test blocks were placed in a curing room for standard curing over a 28-day period, and the concrete prism test blocks can be obtained.

According to the area potential crack emergence during four-point bending test of concrete prisms, a specific 300 mm × 80 mm area located in the center of the prism surface was selected for the application of the GQDs-epoxy resin composite coating, as illustrated in Fig. 16 . This selected area ensured comprehensive coverage for effective crack characterization upon crack initiation. The concrete surface was coated utilizing the method described in Section 4.1. Based on preliminary testing, a coating thickness of 0.5 mm was deemed optimal for monitoring cracks on the concrete surface. Upon completion of curing, four-point bending tests were conducted to induce cracks on the concrete prism surface, enabling subsequent monitoring and analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Four-point bending test device and loading scheme
ce:section.ce:para: A bending test machine with a range of 300 kN (YAW-300/10) was employed in the four-point bending test, depicted in Fig. 17 (a). To realize the generation of cracks, a loading distribution beam was incorporated. The loading rate was maintained at 0.1 MPa/s, with loading ceasing upon significant load fluctuations. Subsequent to the four-point bending test, surface cracks would develop on the concrete specimens. For fluorescence acquisition surrounding the cracks, two degrees of freedom in two directions were essential for precise fluorescence collection. The fluorescence response acquisition system, depicted in Fig. 17(b), featured a vertically downward optical fiber alignment with concrete cracks. Translation of the concrete specimens facilitated the collection of fluorescence emanating from the crack vicinity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Four-point bending test results and discussion
ce:section.ce:para: Two batches of four-point bending tests were conducted, with two specimens per batch, to investigate the fluorescence response of the composites to concrete cracks. Four concrete specimens were labeled: LZT1, LZT2, LZT3, and LZT4, respectively.

Fig. 18 illustrates the concrete cracks observed in LZT1 and LZT2 in the initial test batch. Each of the two concrete prisms exhibited a penetrating crack located centrally, with synchronous crack development in the composite film, aligning with the site of composite material cracking. Four representative points (designated as A, B, C, and D) were selected along each crack for measurement of crack widths using a concrete crack width measuring instrument. The crack width at each of the four points for LZT1 concrete was approximately 0.5 mm, while for LZT2 concrete, it was approximately 0.3 mm. Due to the crack penetration, the width at each point was largely consistent.

The fluorescence of the GQDs-epoxy resin composite film on the surface of concrete cracks was excited by 365 nm UV light, depicted in Fig. 18(b) and Fig. 18(d). A fluorescent bright line corresponding to the concrete crack appeared. This was because the synchronization of the surface composite material cracking with the concrete below, which caused the original GQDs to migrate towards the crack edge, increasing the concentration of GQDs at the crack site, resulting in more GQDs exposure. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity at the crack site was heightened when excited by UV light, the fluorescence effect became more pronounced. Therefore, the composite materials offer improved characterization and positioning capabilities for concrete crack detection.

A fluorescence fiber optic spectrometer was employed to gauge the fluorescence intensity at points A, B, C, and D along the concrete cracks of LZT1 and LZT2, as well as their respective left and right sides, and the alterations in fluorescence response were scrutinized. As depicted in Fig. 19 , the fluorescence intensity at each point demonstrated a higher magnitude at the crack compared to its adjacent left and right sides, specifically, with the maximum being 19.4 % higher. This underscored the commendable fluorescence responsiveness of the GQD-epoxy resin composites towards concrete crack detection, suggesting its efficacy for practical monitoring applications.

To delve deeper, the fluorescence intensity of the composite film at the crack site was measured longitudinally along the direction of the concrete crack, elucidating the correlation between fluorescence intensity and crack width. As illustrated in Fig. 20 (a)–(b), the fluorescence spectra at each point predominantly converged, implying minimal fluctuation across the four points. Furthermore, the spectral peaks were extracted and delineated in Fig. 20(c)-(d). It was observed that as the crack width decreased from point A to point B, the fluorescence intensity augmented, while with an increase in crack width from point B to point C, the fluorescence intensity subsided. This preliminary observation suggested a potential inverse relationship between the crack width and fluorescence intensity. However, the narrow variation range in crack widths across the four points constrained the extent of fluorescence intensity change. Thus, additional experiments were imperative to scrutinize the correlation between crack width and fluorescence intensity further, thereby validating the aforementioned deductions.

To further explore the correlation between fluorescence intensity and crack width, the bending test machine was programmed to halt loading immediately upon load reduction, ensuring the formation of a developing crack, which exhibited a trend of width reduction from wide to narrow.

Fig. 21 illustrates the cracks in LZT3 and LZT4 concrete during the second test batch. These cracks propagated upwards from the bottom of concrete prisms until reaching the top of the composite film area, without forming penetrating cracks. Five distinct points (A, B, C, D, and E) along the crack were identified, and the crack width at each point was measured. The crack width gradually diminished from point A to point E in all concrete prisms. Specifically, the crack width of LZT3 decreased from 0.5 mm to 0.08 mm, and that of LZT4 decreased from 0.55 mm to 0.09 mm, consistent with the behavior of developing cracks. Upon excitation with 365 nm UV light, the composited emitted blue fluorescence, creating a distinct fluorescent line at the crack site. This demonstrated that the GQDs-epoxy resin composites effectively characterize concrete cracks and induce noticeable fluorescence enhancement, whether dealing with penetrating or developing concrete cracks.

The correlation between fluorescence intensity and crack width in the second batch of cracks was explored. As illustrated in Fig. 22 (a)–(b), the five-point fluorescence spectrum peaks of LZT3 and LZT4 concrete cracks exhibited a gradual increase trend, while the crack width from point A to E gradually diminished, indicating a negative correlation between the two variables. To enhance intuitive understanding of this relationship, the spectral peak corresponding to each point was extracted and combined with the crack width, as depicted in Fig. 22(c)–(d). It was evident that fluorescence intensity increased as crack width decreased, suggesting an approximate inverse proportionality between crack width and fluorescence intensity. This can be attributed to two primary factors. Firstly, the fluorescence intensity detected by the optical fiber probe encompassed the total fluorescence within its coverage area, with no fluorescence emitted from within the composites crack. Consequently, as crack width increased, the actual area of fluorescence intensity captured by the optical fiber probe decreased, resulting in an inverse relationship between fluorescence intensity at the crack and crack width, i.e., fluorescence intensity of the composites increased as crack width decreased. The second reason pertained to the viscoelastic nature of the GQDs-epoxy resin composite material. When the cracking initiated, the original composites underwent partial contraction towards both sides of the crack. As the crack developed, the extent of contraction varied. This discrepancy in contraction levels may result in a difference in concentration, with a higher concentration of GQDs at narrower crack widths compared to wider ones. The fluorescence intensity at wider crack widths was lower than that at narrower ones, leading to an inverse relationship between the crack width and the fluorescence intensity of composites. The composite material can effectively monitor and characterize cracks by the enhancement of fluorescence intensity at the crack site, as well as the inverse relationship between fluorescence intensity and crack width. This further underscored the potential of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites for concrete crack monitoring and characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence monitoring mechanism of concrete cracks
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response observed at concrete cracks can be attributed to the fluctuation in the GQDs concentration surrounding the crack. As depicted in Fig. 23 , upon the formation of crack within the concrete, the crack propagated into the GQDs-epoxy resin composite film. The excellent compatibility between the composite film and the concrete ensured simultaneous cracking of the surface film with concrete. Throughout the cracking process, GQDs initially positioned elsewhere gathered around the crack, leading to an increased concentration of GQDs at the crack site and a greater exposure of GQDs. When stimulated by 365 nm UV light, the heightened number of GQDs within the excitation range resulted in an intensified fluorescence intensity. Based on this principle, the occurrence of cracks triggered the generation of a fluorescent bright line on the surface composite film under UV light irradiation, enabling precise crack detection on concrete structures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of fatigue cracks in steel structure
ce:section.ce:para: To be precise, concrete cracks typically manifest as macro cracks at the millimeter level. To explore the suitability of the GQDs-epoxy resin composite material, it was applied to the surface of steel structure to examine the fluorescence response and characterization of micro-fatigue cracks at the micrometer level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of standard compact tensile specimen
ce:section.ce:para: Following the specifications outlined in the Chinese standard GB/T 6398-2017 [53], standard compact tensile steel specimens (CT specimens) were adopted to prefabricate fatigue cracks. To expedite the fabrication of fatigue cracks, a 10 mm - long sharp notch was introduced downward by wire cutting at the triangular notch of CT specimen (Fig. 24 (a)). Q235 steel was employed as the material for CT specimen. The processed CT specimens are depicted in Fig. 24(b). Given the width of fatigue crack of CT specimen was significantly narrower than that of concrete cracks, the thickness of coating film on CT specimen was controlled to approximately 0.3 mm. The coating method aligned with Section 3.1. As illustrated in Fig. 24(c), the coating film was positioned on the right side of each notch, which allowed for smooth expansion of the fatigue crack into the coated area. A total of three sets of CT specimens were coated, with two sets coated with blue fluorescent composite film (CT specimens 1 and 2) and one set with green fluorescent film (CT specimen 3) (Fig. 24(d)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tensile test device and loading scheme
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 25 (a), fatigue cracks on CT specimens were prefabricate using a high-frequency fatigue tensile machine (MTS 809). Firstly, the coated CT specimen was positioned within the fixture, and then wire was inserted into the limit hole to initiate the test by activating the oil pump clamping fixture at both upper and bottom ends. The fatigue loading system of the CT specimen was illustrated in Fig. 25(b). The average load applied was 6.6 kN, with an alternating load amplitude of 5.4 kN, a stress ratio of 0.1, and a cyclic frequency of 15 Hz.

The progression of fatigue crack formation was continuously monitored throughout the loading process. When the fatigue cracks expanded to the GQDs-epoxy resin composite film, the loading was halted, and the CT sample was removed for investigating the fluorescence response of the composite film to the fatigue cracks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tensile test results and discussion
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 26 (a),(c), when the fatigue cracks extended and entered composite films, the concurrent cracking of the films was induced, resulting in a slender, bright fluorescent line. This phenomenon mirrored the cracking behavior observed in the coated concrete, suggesting that the GQDs-epoxy resin composite material also exhibited heightened fluorescence sensitivity to cracks at the micrometer level. Fig. 26(b) displays the CT specimen subjected to direct pulling apart, revealing the simultaneous separation of the blue fluorescent composite film, accompanied by fluorescence intensification at the crack periphery. It can be concluded that the 0.3 mm-thick composite film maintained close correspondence with the fatigue crack progression in the CT steel specimen, generating a fluorescence enhancement phenomenon (fluorescent bright line) around the crack.

The width of cracks was measured by a crack width measuring instrument capable of 150x magnification (with a minimum observation width of 5 μm). Fig. 26(a) and Fig. 26(c) illustrate the crack widths of both specimens 1 and 3 coated with blue and green composite films post-cracking. Four reference points A, B, C, and D were marked along the cracks in the green fluorescent film, while only three reference points A, B, and C were marked for the shorter crack in the blue fluorescent composite film. The crack width reduced from 60 μm to 10 μm between points A and C of the blue fluorescent composite film, and from 110 μm to 20 μm between points A and D of the green fluorescent composite film, indicating a decreasing trend in width consistent with fatigue crack development principles. Utilizing this width measurement effectively characterized the crack, facilitating subsequent fluorescence response analysis.

The conclusion drawn in Section 5.1.3 was that the appearance and expansion of concrete cracks resulted in a variation in fluorescence intensity within the crack area, with the fluorescence intensity at the crack site being higher than that on its adjacent left and right sides. Regarding fatigue cracks in CT steel specimens, measurements of fluorescence intensity were conducted at the crack site and its adjacent left and right sides, taking the green composite film as an example. As depicted in Fig. 27 , the fluorescence intensity at the crack site exceeded that of its adjacent left and right sides, specifically, with the maximum being 31.5 % higher, consistent with the conclusion of Section 5.1.3.

Further exploration was conducted on the correlation between fluorescence intensity and crack width of specimens 1 and 3 coated with blue and green composite films. As illustrated in Fig. 28 (a) and (b), the fluorescence peak at the crack site showed an upward trend with the decrease of the crack width. Furthermore, the spectral peak corresponding to each point was extracted and combined with the crack width, as depicted in Fig. 28(c) and (d). It was observed that fluorescence intensity increased as crack width decreased, indicating an approximate inverse proportionality between the two variables. This aligned with the observed phenomenon in concrete crack monitoring, suggesting that composite material can effectively monitor microscopic fatigue cracks.

Additionally, this material exhibited a notable fluorescence response even for cracks at the micrometer level that were invisible to the naked eye, so the GQDs-epoxy composite material has the practicality of practical crack monitoring applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence monitoring mechanism of steel fatigue cracks
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 29 provides a more intuitive depiction of the fluorescence monitoring mechanism of the GQDs-epoxy resin composites to steel fatigue cracks. Fatigue cracks will appear in the CT specimen under fatigue load. Based on the excellent synchronization between the GQDs-epoxy resin composite film and the surface of the specimen, as the fatigue cracks propagate into the composite film will drive it to crack together. During the cracking process, the movement of GQDs towards the crack periphery due to strain induction, resulting in a denser arrangement of GQDs and an increased concentration per unit area. Under the UV light irradiation, a distinct fluorescence line emerges. The above clarified the primary mechanism behind the fluorescence response of the GQDs-epoxy composites to fatigue cracks. Measurement of fluorescence intensity at the crack site via a fiber optic spectrometer reveals higher intensity compared to its adjacent left and right sides. Additionally, the crack width demonstrates an approximate inverse relationship with fluorescence intensity, i.e., narrower cracks exhibit higher fluorescence intensity along the cracking direction. The above two fluorescence response characteristics of the composite material effectively characterize the presence of fatigue cracks. If implemented practically, the GQDs-epoxy resin composites are poised to deliver an efficient fluorescence response during the initial stages of crack formation, facilitating timely monitoring and early warning.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, the GQDs-epoxy resin composites were formulated to applied on the structural surface to explore the feasibility of monitoring the surface strain and cracks of the structure. Through tensile, compression, bending, and fatigue experiments, the relationship between structural strain and fluorescence response was established. Furthermore, the fluorescence response of the composites to the cracks were studied. The conclusions are drawn as follows:
(1)
Two types of GQDs with excellent properties were selected. GQDs-epoxy resin composites were successfully fabricated, with a high transparent epoxy resin as the carrier matrix and anhydrous ethanol as the solvent for GQDs. The preparation procedure of the composites was standardized, and a volume ratio standard of epoxy resin to curing agent to GQDs solution at 2.5: 1: 0.9 was established.
(2)
During both single and multiple cyclic loading tests of coated steel specimens, the fluorescence intensity of the composite material exhibited two types of positive and negative correlation responses with the increasing of the strain. These two responses were respectively caused by the strain-induced reduction in the number of GQDs per unit area and the strain-induced separation of aggregated GQDs. The composites had an excellent fluorescence response to tensile strain.
(3)
During the elastic stage, as the strength grade of coated concrete specimens increased, the growth of structural compressive strain and fluorescence intensity slowed down, and the maximum structural compressive strain and maximum fluorescence intensity decreased. Over time, both the fitted structural compressive strain and the fitted relative fluorescence intensity demonstrated an increase, and the relationship between the two was predominantly linear. The composites exhibited a heightened fluorescence sensitivity to compressive strain.
(4)
The composites demonstrated excellent crack characterization and fluorescence response effects on concrete and steel structures, whether dealing with penetrating or developing cracks, showcasing their potential for structural crack monitoring. The fluorescence response of the composites to cracks was based on the fluorescence enhancement phenomenon (fluorescence enhancement bright line) at the crack site, with the fluorescence intensity at each crack point surpassed that on the adjacent left and right sides. Additionally, the composites exhibited a notable fluorescence response even for the cracks at the micrometer level that were invisible to the naked eye.
(5)
For developing cracks, the fluorescence intensity of the composites was approximately inversely proportional to the crack width. Along the cracking direction, the fluorescence intensity increased with the decrease of the crack width. The GQDs-epoxy resin composites are poised to deliver an efficient fluorescence response during the initial stages of structural crack formation, facilitating timely monitoring and early warning.
